full_text:
The Impact of Sleep Deprivation on Academic
Performance
James Adeyemi, Fatima Al-Hassan, Lucas Mendes
Department of Cognitive Science, University of Lagos   March 2024
Abstract
Sleep deprivation is increasingly recognised as a significant public health challenge among
university students. This study investigates the relationship between sleep duration, sleep quality,
and academic performance across a sample of 412 undergraduate students at the University of
Lagos over one academic semester. Using validated instruments including the Pittsburgh Sleep
Quality Index (PSQI) and official grade point averages (GPA), we find that students sleeping fewer
than six hours per night scored an average of 0.74 GPA points lower than those sleeping seven to
nine hours. Multivariate regression analysis controlling for gender, year of study, and part-time
employment confirms that sleep duration is a statistically significant independent predictor of
academic outcomes (p < 0.001). These findings have important implications for university wellness
programmes and academic scheduling policy.
1. Introduction
Adequate sleep is essential for cognitive function, memory consolidation, and emotional
regulation. Despite this, surveys consistently show that between 60 and 70 percent of university
students report insufficient sleep on a regular basis. Factors contributing to poor sleep in this
population include academic workload, social activities, part-time employment, digital device use,
and irregular schedules arising from the flexibility of university life.
Previous research in North American and European contexts has established a robust association
between poor sleep and reduced academic performance. However, comparatively little work has
examined this relationship within Sub-Saharan African university settings, where students may
face additional stressors including commuting distances, power outages affecting study
environments, and financial pressures. This study addresses that gap by examining sleep and
academic outcomes in a large Nigerian university cohort.
We hypothesise that (H1) shorter sleep duration will be associated with lower GPA, and (H2)
poorer subjective sleep quality will independently predict academic underperformance even after
controlling for total sleep time.
2. Methods
Participants were recruited via stratified random sampling across all six faculties of the University
of Lagos during the 2023/2024 academic year. A total of 412 undergraduate students (217 female,

195 male; mean age 20.4 years, SD = 1.8) completed the study. Inclusion criteria required full-time
enrolment and no diagnosed sleep disorder.
Sleep was assessed using two measures. Objective sleep duration was estimated via a seven-day
wrist actigraphy protocol using Philips Actiwatch devices. Subjective sleep quality was measured
using the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI), a validated 19-item self-report questionnaire
yielding a global score from 0 to 21, with scores above five indicating poor sleep quality.
Academic performance was operationalised as cumulative GPA at the end of the semester,
obtained with written consent from the academic registry. Statistical analyses were conducted in R
4.3.1. We used Pearson correlations for bivariate relationships and hierarchical multiple regression
for multivariate analyses. Significance threshold was set at alpha = 0.05.
3. Results
The mean nightly sleep duration across the sample was 5.9 hours (SD = 1.2). Only 31 percent of
participants met the recommended minimum of seven hours per night. Mean PSQI score was 7.3
(SD = 2.6), indicating that the majority of participants experienced poor sleep quality by standard
clinical thresholds.
Bivariate analysis revealed a significant positive correlation between sleep duration and GPA (r =
0.48, p < 0.001). Students in the lowest sleep quartile (less than 5 hours) had a mean GPA of
2.61, compared to 3.35 for students in the highest quartile (7 or more hours). PSQI global score
was negatively correlated with GPA (r = -0.39, p < 0.001), confirming that subjective sleep quality
independently predicted performance.
The hierarchical regression model explained 38 percent of variance in GPA (adjusted R2 = 0.38).
In the final model, sleep duration (beta = 0.31, p < 0.001) and PSQI score (beta = -0.22, p < 0.001)
remained significant after controlling for gender, year of study, and part-time employment status.
Female students showed marginally higher GPA on average (beta = 0.09, p = 0.04), and third-year
students outperformed first-year students (beta = 0.14, p = 0.01).
4. Discussion
Our findings confirm and extend prior work by demonstrating that sleep deprivation significantly
undermines academic performance in a West African university context. The effect size observed
(approximately 0.74 GPA points between lowest and highest sleep quartiles) is comparable to that
reported in North American studies, suggesting the relationship is robust across cultural and
institutional settings.
The independent contribution of sleep quality beyond sleep duration is a noteworthy finding.
Students who spend adequate time in bed but report fragmented or non-restorative sleep are still
academically disadvantaged. This implies that intervention programmes should address sleep
hygiene and sleep disorders, not merely encourage earlier bedtimes.

5. Conclusion
This study provides strong evidence that sleep deprivation negatively impacts academic
performance among Nigerian university students. Both sleep duration and sleep quality are
independent predictors of GPA. Universities should consider embedding sleep health education
into orientation programmes, revising examination scheduling to avoid early morning slots, and
providing access to evidence-based cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) through
student wellness centres. Future research should employ longitudinal designs to establish causal
directionality and examine the role of napping behaviour as a compensatory strategy.
References
[1] Buysse, D. J., Reynolds, C. F., Monk, T. H., Berman, S. R., & Kupfer, D. J. (1989). The Pittsburgh Sleep
Quality Index: A new instrument for psychiatric practice and research. Psychiatry Research, 28(2), 193 213.
[2] Curcio, G., Ferrara, M., & De Gennaro, L. (2006). Sleep loss, learning capacity and academic
performance. Sleep Medicine Reviews, 10(5), 323 337.
[3] Onyeka, I. N., & Obi, C. (2021). Sleep patterns and academic achievement among Nigerian
undergraduates: A cross-sectional study. African Journal of Health Sciences, 34(1), 45 58.
[4] Walker, M. (2017). Why We Sleep: Unlocking the Power of Sleep and Dreams. Scribner.
[5] Lund, H. G., Reider, B. D., Whiting, A. B., & Prichard, J. R. (2010). Sleep patterns and predictors of
disturbed sleep in a large population of college students. Journal of Adolescent Health, 46(2), 124 132.

This study addresses that gap by examining sleep and academic outcomes in a large Nigerian university cohort. We hypothesise that (H1) shorter sleep duration will be associated with lower GPA, and (H2) poorer subjective sleep quality will independently predict academic underperformance even after controlling for total sleep time.

Participants were recruited via stratified random sampling across all six faculties of the University of Lagos during the 2023/2024 academic year. The mean nightly sleep duration across the sample was 5.9 hours (SD = 1.2). Only 31 percent of participants met the recommended minimum of seven hours per night. Mean PSQI score was 7.3 (SD = 2.6), indicating that the majority of participants experienced poor sleep quality by standard clinical thresholds.

The effect size observed (approximately 0.74 GPA points between lowest and highest sleep quartiles) is comparable to that reported in North American studies, suggesting the relationship is robust across cultural and institutional settings. The independent contribution of sleep quality beyond sleep duration is a noteworthy finding. Both sleep duration and sleep quality are independent predictors of GPA.
